@graph.name: Land Rover Range Rover Evoque P250 Engine Era Map
Land Rover Range Rover Evoque P250 Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Range Rover Evoque P250 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 204PT engine typically costs £3,000–£5,000 supply only, with used units available from £1,500–£2,800. Fitting adds £1,300–£1,800 depending on the workshop. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your Evoque.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Range Rover Evoque P250 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes – the 204PT petrol Ingenium is considered more reliable than its diesel counterparts. It has fewer chronic issues; the main concerns are cooling system components (water pump) and occasional timing chain tensioner wear. With good maintenance, these engines are dependable.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Range Rover Evoque P250?

acceptedAnswer.text: Water pump and cooling system leaks, timing chain tensioner wear (less common than on diesel Ingenium), and PCV valve failure leading to increased oil consumption. All are well‑understood by JLR specialists.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an Evoque P250 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Evoque values remain strong — a well‑kept L551 is often worth £18,000–£35,000+. A £3,000–£5,000 reconditioned engine is only 10–20% of the vehicle's value — well within the Land Rover 60% scrap threshold. Replacement is almost always worthwhile.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Land Rover Range Rover Evoque P250 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is 204PT — it's the only petrol four‑cylinder used in the L551 Evoque P250. You can also find it on the VIN plate or in the vehicle documentation.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the 204PT timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes – if caught early, the tensioner and chain can be replaced preventatively (£1,200–£2,000). If the chain jumps and causes valve interference, a full engine replacement (£3,000+) is usually required. Act on any cold‑start rattle.

eraMap.title: The P250 THROUGH TIME — Era Map

replacementCosts.h2: Land Rover Range Rover Evoque P250 Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current Evoque P250 generation. This badge uses only the 204PT engine — no era ambiguity.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an Evoque P250 engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer).

cta.label: → Compare P250 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Water Pump and Cooling System Leaks
problems.symptoms: Gradual coolant loss, dampness around the front of the engine, rising temperature gauge, occasional warning light
problems.recommendation: The 204PT's cooling system is its most common weak point. The water pump and thermostat housing are known to develop leaks, especially on higher‑mileage examples. Catching it early avoids overheating damage. Replace the water pump as a preventative measure at around 60,000 miles – it's a well‑understood job for any JLR specialist.

failureLink.label: Read the full coolant leak guide

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start for 1–2 seconds, occasional engine warning light if stretch alters cam timing
problems.replacementCost: £3,000–£5,000 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)
problems.recommendation: Unlike the diesel Ingenium, the 204PT's timing chain issues are less catastrophic and less frequent, but they do occur. The tensioner can wear over time. A cold‑start rattle that lasts more than a second or two should be investigated promptly. Preventative replacement at around 80,000–100,000 miles is cheaper than a full engine rebuild.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: PCV Valve and Oil Consumption
problems.symptoms: Increased oil consumption, blue smoke on startup, occasional rough idle
problems.recommendation: The PCV (positive crankcase ventilation) valve can fail, causing oil to be drawn into the intake. This leads to increased oil consumption and potentially carbon build‑up. Replacement is inexpensive and straightforward. Monitor your oil level between services – if it drops noticeably, have the PCV checked.

failureLink.label: Read the full PCV valve failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Evoque P250 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Cost‑effective on a well‑maintained example; cheaper than a full engine
Valve/piston contact means rebuild or replace
Simple and cheap — don't ignore oil consumption
Viable on low‑mileage or cherished examples

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Evoque P250? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented water pump replacement or coolant system service history
* No rattles on cold start – a quiet start-up suggests healthy timing chain tension
* Full service history with correct JLR‑spec oil (0W‑20)
* Evidence of regular maintenance — the 204PT rewards good care
avoidIf: * Evidence of repeated coolant top‑ups without a diagnosed leak
* Cold‑start rattle that lasts more than a couple of seconds
* Poor service history – these engines are sensitive to oil quality
* Any engine warning light without a clear diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 204PT petrol is a solid engine that avoids the major reliability pitfalls of the diesel Ingenium. Its primary weaknesses are cooling system components and, occasionally, timing chain tensioners. A well‑serviced P250 with a fresh water pump is a dependable long‑term proposition. The engine is modern, refined, and parts availability is strong across the UK. This is the sweet spot in the Evoque range for those seeking performance and reliability in equal measure.

cta.label: → Compare P250 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Range Rover Evoque P250 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Listen for rattles lasting more than 1–2 seconds (timing chain tensioner warning)
2. Service History — including JLR‑spec oil changes (0W‑20) every 16,000 miles or annually
3. Coolant Level — any signs of leaks around the water pump or thermostat housing?
4. Oil Consumption — does it use more than 0.5L per 1,000 miles? (PCV may be failing)
5. Engine Warning Light — have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
6. MOT History — check for emissions‑related failures (catalyst, O2 sensors)
7. Turbo/Boost — test acceleration; any hesitation or whistling? (turbo issues are rare but possible)
8. Transmission — the 9-speed ZF auto is generally robust; ensure smooth shifts

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly — one engine throughout this badge's history.
